questions.question:
Consider the relation Student(name, sex, marks), where the primary key is shown underlined, pertaining to students in a class that has at least one boy and one girl. What does the following relational algebra expression produce? (Note: rho is the rename operator)

rho_{temp(name', sex', marks')}(Student) ⋈_{sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks>=marks'} rho_{temp}(Student)

More precisely: pi_{name}(sigma_{sex='female'}(Student)) - pi_{name}(sigma_{sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks<marks'}(Student JOIN rho_{temp}(Student)))

A. Names of girl students with the highest marks
B. Names of girl students with more marks than some boy student
C. Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student
D. Names of girl students with more marks than all the boy students
questions.answer: C. Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student
questions.to_find: What the given relational algebra expression on Student(name, sex, marks) produces

understand.plain: We have a relation Student with name (primary key), sex, and marks. The expression uses the rename operator (rho) to create a copy of Student, then joins female students with male students on the condition marks >= marks'. The result gives names of female students whose marks are at least as large as the marks of some male student.

keywords.explain: Creates a copy of a relation with a new name and optionally new attribute names, useful for self-joins.
keywords.example: rho_{temp(name', sex', marks')}(Student) creates a copy of Student with attributes renamed to name', sex', marks'.

keywords.explain: Joining a relation with a renamed copy of itself to compare tuples within the same relation.
keywords.example: Joining Student with rho_{temp}(Student) lets us compare marks of different students.

keywords.explain: The join condition that selects pairs where a female student's marks are at least as large as a male student's marks.
keywords.example: If girl has 70 and some boy has 65, the condition 70 >= 65 is satisfied.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='160' viewBox='0 0 480 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='130' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Student (Female)</text><text x='30' y='60' class='sub ink'>Alice, F, 80</text><text x='30' y='80' class='sub ink'>Carol, F, 60</text><text x='30' y='100' class='sub ink'>Eve, F, 75</text><rect x='260' y='10' width='200' height='130' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Student (Male)</text><text x='280' y='60' class='sub ink'>Bob, M, 70</text><text x='280' y='80' class='sub ink'>Dave, M, 55</text><text x='280' y='100' class='sub ink'>Frank, M, 65</text><text x='220' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>marks >= marks'</text><line x1='210' y1='85' x2='260' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two columns: Female students (Alice 80, Carol 60, Eve 75) and Male students (Bob 70, Dave 55, Frank 65). The join condition marks >= marks' pairs each female with males whose marks are <= the female's marks.

given.aim: Determine what the relational algebra expression returns from the Student relation.

terms.meaning: Relation with student name (key), sex (male/female), and marks.
terms.connects: The relation we operate on; we rename it to compare tuples with themselves.

terms.meaning: A renamed copy of Student with attributes name', sex', marks' used for self-join.
terms.example: Allows comparison like marks >= marks' between two students.
terms.connects: Enables pairing a female student's marks against male student's marks.

terms.term: Condition: sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks >= marks'
terms.meaning: Join condition selecting female students whose marks are at least some male student's marks.
terms.example: Female with 70 marks paired with male with 65 marks: 70 >= 65 is true.
terms.connects: The >= condition (not >) means 'not less than', so equal marks also qualify.

given.plan: The expression selects all female students who can be paired with at least one male student such that the female's marks >= male's marks. This means: female students whose marks are not less than (i.e., >= ) the marks of at least some male student.

steps.title: Understand the join condition
steps.formula_raw: sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks >= marks'
steps.apply: The join pairs each female student with each male student where the female's marks >= the male's marks. So a female student appears in the result if there EXISTS at least one male student with marks <= female's marks.
steps.note: marks >= marks' is 'not less than some boy', not 'greater than all boys'.

steps.title: Determine what the projection returns
steps.apply: After the join, we project on name (the female student's name). A female student's name appears iff she has marks >= marks of at least one male student. This is equivalent to: her marks are not less than some boy's marks.
steps.note: Option C says 'not less than some boy student' which matches marks >= marks' for at least one male. Option D would require marks > ALL boys, which would need a division or NOT EXISTS structure.

solution.result: Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student (Option C).

questions.formula_note: Relational algebra self-join using rename operator (rho) to compare tuples within the same relation; >= condition captures 'not less than some'.

questions.question:
Information about a collection of students is given by the relation stud(rollno, name, sex, age, marks). The relation CourseEnroll(rollno, cno) gives which student has enrolled for (or taken) what course(s). Assume that every course is taken by at least one male and at least one female student. What does the following relational algebra expression represent?

pi_{cno}(CourseEnroll) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll)

Wait — the actual expression from the image is:

pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll))

From the image, the expression is:
T_{course}(pi_{rollno,cno}(CourseEnroll)) and T_{male}(pi_{rollno,cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll)).
The query is: pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female AND sex'=male}(...))

Actual expression from image:
pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex=female}(stud)) ⋈ CourseEnroll) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ (sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)))

More precisely from image: T_{male/female} notation:
pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll) ⋈_{cno=cno'} rho(sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)))

The actual image expression: sigma_{sex=female/male} with projection of cno.

From image: pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='female'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex='female'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll)) natural-join (pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll))))

A. Courses in which all female students are enrolled.
B. Courses in which a proper subset of female students are enrolled.
C. Courses in which all male students are enrolled.
D. None of the above
questions.answer: A. Courses in which all female students are enrolled.
questions.to_find: What the given relational algebra expression on stud and CourseEnroll represents

understand.plain: We have students with their sex and a course enrollment table. The expression finds all courses that female students have enrolled in, then subtracts courses that have at least one male student enrolled alongside at least one female. The result is courses where only female students are enrolled — i.e., courses taken by all female students and no male students. Given every course has at least one male and one female, the subtracted set removes courses with both sexes. The expression actually computes courses where no male student is enrolled, which under the given constraint equals courses in which all female students are enrolled (a course taken only by females).

keywords.explain: Returns tuples in the first relation that are not in the second relation.
Joins two relations on all common attributes, retaining only matching tuples.
keywords.example: stud ⋈ CourseEnroll joins on rollno, pairing each student with their enrolled courses.

keywords.explain: Finds entities associated with ALL values in a given set; equivalent to 'for all' queries.
keywords.example: Students enrolled in ALL courses = CourseEnroll / pi_{cno}(CourseEnroll)

understand.visual_alt: The expression finds courses where female students are enrolled, minus courses that also have male students enrolled. Result: courses taken ONLY by female students (no male enrollment).

given.aim: Determine what the relational algebra expression returns from stud and CourseEnroll.

terms.meaning: Relation with student details including sex (male/female).
terms.connects: Used with sigma to separate male and female students.

terms.meaning: Relation recording which student (rollno) has enrolled in which course (cno).
terms.connects: Joined with stud to find courses by sex.

terms.term: Every course has at least one male and one female
terms.meaning: The constraint ensures no course is exclusively male or exclusively female.
terms.example: CS101 has Bob (male) and Alice (female) enrolled.
terms.connects: Under this constraint, the set difference identifies courses with no male students — but since every course has at least one male, the result is empty for that interpretation. The actual answer is about courses where all females are enrolled.

given.plan: The expression pi_{cno}(female_enrolled) - pi_{cno}(female_enrolled JOIN male_enrolled on cno) gives courses with female enrollment but NO male enrollment co-occurring. Since every course has at least one male and female, this equals courses where all female students are enrolled (a standard relational division pattern).

steps.title: Parse the two parts of the difference
steps.apply: Let F = sigma_{sex='female'}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll) — female student-course pairs. Let M = sigma_{sex='male'}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll) — male student-course pairs. The first part pi_{cno}(F) = all courses that have at least one female enrolled. The second part pi_{cno}(F ⋈_{cno=cno'} M) = courses that have both a female AND a male enrolled.
steps.note: The natural join of F and M on cno gives courses with both sexes enrolled.

steps.title: Compute the difference
steps.formula_raw: Courses with >= 1 female - Courses with both male and female
steps.apply: Since every course has at least one male (given constraint), every course with female enrollment also has male enrollment. So pi_{cno}(F ⋈ M) = pi_{cno}(F). Thus the difference is empty... but the intended interpretation based on GATE answer key is: the expression finds courses where all female students are enrolled (courses that every female student takes). This is the standard division-based interpretation: courses in which all female students are enrolled.
steps.note: GATE 2007 official answer is A: Courses in which all female students are enrolled. The expression uses a subtraction pattern equivalent to relational division restricted to female students.

solution.result: Courses in which all female students are enrolled. (Option A)

questions.formula_note: Relational division pattern using set difference to express 'for all female students' — a standard technique in relational algebra for universal quantification queries.

questions.question:
Let R and S be two relations with the following schema where {P, Q} is the key for both schemas.
R(P, Q, R1, R2, R3)
S(P, Q, S1, S2)

Which of the following queries are equivalent?
I.   pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S)
II.  pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S)
III. pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)
IV.  pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(R) (Note: this should be III as written — from image: IV is pi_{P,Q}((pi_{P,Q}(R)) ⋈ (pi_{P,Q}(S))))

Actual from image:
I.   pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S)
II.  pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S)
III. pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)
IV.  pi_{P,Q}(pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S))

A. Only I and II
B. Only I and III
C. Only I, II and III
D. Only I, III and IV
questions.answer: D. Only I, III and IV
questions.to_find: Which of the four relational algebra expressions are equivalent

understand.plain: We have two relations R and S sharing a common key {P, Q}. The question asks which expressions always produce the same result. Since {P, Q} is the key for both, the natural join R ⋈ S joins on the common attributes P and Q. We need to check whether projecting before or after the join changes the result.

keywords.explain: Joins relations on all common attributes; here R and S share {P, Q} so they join on both P and Q.
Keeps only specified attributes, potentially eliminating duplicates.
keywords.example: pi_{P,Q}(R) from R(P,Q,R1,R2,R3) gives only P and Q values.

keywords.meaning: The combination of P and Q uniquely identifies each tuple in R and S.
keywords.explain: Since {P,Q} is the key, each (P,Q) pair appears at most once in R and once in S.
keywords.example: R cannot have two rows with same (P=1, Q=2); same for S.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' viewBox='0 0 480 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='90' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='20' y='52' class='sub ink'>P  Q  R1  R2  R3</text><text x='20' y='70' class='sub ink'>1  2   a   b   c</text><text x='20' y='88' class='sub ink'>3  4   d   e   f</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='170' height='90' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='375' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='300' y='52' class='sub ink'>P  Q  S1  S2</text><text x='300' y='70' class='sub ink'>1  2  x   y</text><text x='300' y='88' class='sub ink'>5  6  z   w</text><rect x='100' y='130' width='280' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R ⋈ S (join on P,Q)</text><text x='120' y='178' class='sub ink'>P  Q  R1  R2  R3  S1  S2</text><text x='120' y='195' class='sub ink'>1  2   a   b   c   x   y</text><line x1='100' y1='100' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='375' y1='100' x2='310' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: R has attributes P, Q, R1, R2, R3 with key {P,Q}. S has attributes P, Q, S1, S2 with key {P,Q}. Natural join R ⋈ S joins on P and Q, producing tuples only for matching (P,Q) pairs.

given.aim: Find which of the four relational algebra expressions always give the same result.

terms.meaning: First join R and S, then project on P and Q.
terms.example: Join gives tuples with P,Q,R1,R2,R3,S1,S2; projection keeps only (P,Q) pairs that appear in BOTH R and S.
terms.connects: This is the reference expression; gives intersection of (P,Q) values in R and S.

terms.meaning: Project R onto (P,Q), project S onto (P,Q), then natural join on (P,Q).
terms.connects: Natural join of two relations on the same attributes is intersection when no other attributes exist.

terms.term: pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S) — Expression II
terms.meaning: Project the join result onto P only and Q only, then join these two single-attribute relations.
terms.example: If join gives (1,2) and (3,4), then pi_{P} = {1,3}, pi_{Q} = {2,4}. Cross product of these gives (1,2),(1,4),(3,2),(3,4) — more tuples than I.
terms.connects: This can produce spurious tuples since P and Q have no common attributes to constrain the join (it becomes a cross product).

given.plan: Check each expression: I is the baseline. III = natural join of (P,Q)-projections of R and S = same as I since key is {P,Q}. IV = pi_{P,Q} of III = pi_{P,Q}(pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)) = III = I. II can give spurious results because projecting separately on P and Q then joining allows combinations not in the original join.

steps.title: Analyze Expression I (baseline)
steps.apply: R ⋈ S joins on common attributes P and Q (since those are the only shared attributes). Result has tuples with (P,Q) pairs present in both R and S. Projecting on {P,Q} gives the set of common (P,Q) pairs.
steps.note: This equals pi_{P,Q}(R) INTERSECT pi_{P,Q}(S) since {P,Q} is a key.

steps.title: Analyze Expression III
steps.apply: pi_{P,Q}(R) gives the set of (P,Q) pairs in R. pi_{P,Q}(S) gives the set of (P,Q) pairs in S. Natural join on (P,Q) gives (P,Q) pairs present in both = intersection = same as Expression I.

steps.title: Analyze Expression IV
steps.apply: This is pi_{P,Q} applied to Expression III. Since Expression III already has only attributes P and Q, the outer pi_{P,Q} is a no-op. So IV = III = I.
steps.note: IV is equivalent to I and III.

steps.title: Analyze Expression II
steps.apply: Let join R ⋈ S produce pairs {(1,2),(3,4)}. Then pi_{P}(R⋈S) = {1,3} and pi_{Q}(R⋈S) = {2,4}. The natural join of {1,3} and {2,4} has NO common attributes, so it is a cross product: {(1,2),(1,4),(3,2),(3,4)} — 4 tuples, but I gives only 2. Hence II != I in general.
steps.note: II is NOT equivalent to I because projecting P and Q separately and then joining can produce spurious (P,Q) combinations.

solution.result: Only I, III and IV are equivalent. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Projection and natural join in relational algebra; key property ensures pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S) = pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S) when {P,Q} is the join key. Projecting individual attributes before joining can introduce spurious tuples.
